title: New Evangelion Thesis
wikitext:
[]: New Evangelion Thesis             Quote Post by JohnyPanic » Tue Apr 09, 2013 7:42 am Hey Guys, I've recently finished my thesis on Evangelion and I though it would be nice if someone check this out and possibly comment, critisize or else. Here is the link http://ru.scribd.com/doc/134877860/Evangelion-Thesis

[]:             Quote Post by A.T. Fish » Tue Apr 09, 2013 9:50 am I like the initiative, but this seems like an academic work (a thesis for a master's degree in psychology I think? I couldn't get much from the polish text) and as such I wouldn't recommend using material from fansites and forums as references as they're not reliable sources by academic standards.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Apr 09, 2013 12:23 pm There's some great quotes in here, but could you provide a non-scribd pdf download of the file? It's hard to read in a browser, and some of us are without Facebook accounts.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Apr 09, 2013 3:58 pm Having read most of this, I found the discussion of Evangelion in the context of Otaku culture to be quite interesting. I got somewhat lost amidst the psychoanalysis/religious references. The main thesis is a little long to digest, but I do have a few suggestions here. Complex Characters - The analysis of Shinji's hand symbolism as representing his anxiety about castration is I believe rather unique. - Asuka does not actually have a doll. You may be think of the doll that the Angel's avatar is holding(and later turns into) during #23'. - Also, Asuka is afraid of her mother because it's very likely that Naoko did ask Asuka to die with her at one point. Naoko went insane after her contact experiment with Eva 02 - As to Misato's "slutty" outfits, note that she wears her Nerv Uniform through most of the show. Admittedly the uniform takes liberties, but OK. - You mention that Asuka is clumsy. I'm not sure if that's the case. Episode #09 suggests she actually has some poise. - You mention the Hedgehog's dilemma, but I don't think you mention that Episode #04 is called Hedgehog's Dilemma, or that it is explicitly mentioned in the show. Father Since I've been thinking a bit about the Shinji/Gendo relationship lately, I found a few observations in this section interesting. - On the matter of Gendo being a sadistic super ego. Certainly Shinji could view him as sadistic, but it's not as certain whether Gendo is actually being sadistic or not. (Unless you're going with th idea of Tokyo-3 all being a construction of Shinji's unconscious) - Note that Gendo and Shinji do have contact other than extramural orders. The scene in #15 by Yui's graveside is absolutely key here. There are vestiges of an underlying relationship still present between the two. I think. Mandala of Individuation - Your assumption that Eva 00 is attacking Gendo and Rei in Episodes #5 and #14 is incorrect. Eva 00 was going after Ritsuko on both occassions. See for example this wiki page . - Also, I found the analysis of the scenes in #19 to be compelling. You may be interested in recent thread on the Bardiel fight as well . - You also appear to have picked up on Misato's approach to Shinji in episode #23', but do you elaborate on this at all? - You've also discussed the hermaphroditic nature of The combined Lilith/Adam/Rei/Kaworu/GNR (I forget what we decided to call this). Reichu will be pleased, about that part anyway. There's a lot of interesting observations in this essay, though we don't often go into psychoanalysis as deeply as this.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Tue Apr 09, 2013 4:07 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: it's very likely that Naoko did ask Asuka to die with her at one point. Naoko went insane after her contact experiment with Eva 02

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Apr 09, 2013 6:07 pm Thanks for the correction. Keeping track of mother's in Evangelion can be tricky at times. Also, I forgot to mention this novel gem from the Thesis. JohnyPanic wrote: The best example of these correlations between functions could be seen in the NGE’s Control Deck. (fig.8) Here we have three lower platforms that contain the three Magisupercomputers that are literary responsible for the rational thinking: starting from analyzing the data from Evangelion and ending with governing the presidential election of Tokyo-3 as mentioned in episode 11. The Magi are controlled by the three members of the personnel at the fourth higher platform, which is fully subjected to the unquestionable and equally unfathomable will of Commander Gendo Ikari. And, even when the main operator of the Magi - the daughter of its founder - Ritsuko Akagi, tries to pull a diversion against Gendo in EOE, one of the three computers inevitably betrays her, showing its inclination towards spontaneity and its allegiance to Gendo. Who despite his apparent superior position, oxymoronically represents the inferior function, taking into the account that NERV is assumed to be the kingdom of Shinji’s unconscious (Napier 2007 84), and the inferior function in this terrain is accordingly regarded as superior.

[]:             Quote Post by JohnyPanic » Wed Apr 10, 2013 3:55 am ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: There's some great quotes in here, but could you provide a non-scribd pdf download of the file? It's hard to read in a browser, and some of us are without Facebook accounts.

[]:             Quote Post by jimmiel » Wed Apr 10, 2013 6:21 am Mate, Gunbuster in 1988 was Anno's directorial debut, not Nadia in 1990-1.

[]:             Quote Post by JohnyPanic » Wed Apr 10, 2013 6:30 am jimmiel wrote: Mate, Gunbuster in 1988 was Anno's directorial debut, not Nadia in 1990-1.

[]:             Quote Post by jimmiel » Wed Apr 10, 2013 6:56 am It seemed like kind of a weird oversight with the research that's gone into this, yeah.

[]:             Quote Post by notalive » Wed Apr 10, 2013 10:05 am While there are some errors (for example you have written that the First Impact was the Big Bang, while it actually was the Black Moon crashing into Earth), some things certainly are a stretch, but otherwise big kudos to you for writing a quality paper on Evangelion. And also: Dobra robota!

[]:             Quote Post by JohnyPanic » Wed Apr 10, 2013 12:02 pm notalive wrote: While there are some errors (for example you have written that the First Impact was the Big Bang, while it actually was the Black Moon crashing into Earth), some things certainly are a stretch, but otherwise big kudos to you for writing a quality paper on Evangelion. And also: Dobra robota!

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Wed Apr 10, 2013 12:53 pm Damn, boy. That's a quality paper, right there. A few mistakes, but that's some cool stuff.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Wed Apr 10, 2013 6:08 pm JohnyPanic wrote: Vocabulary confusion: If I change the word doll for the stuffed animal would it be ok? fig n.2 clearly illustrates what I meant. .... I'm positive that Kyoko never talked to Asuka again since the experiment, she substituted her for a doll as implied by hospital scene, whom she asks to die with her.

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Wed Apr 10, 2013 8:29 pm This looks like you put a lot of work into it. I'll have to digest the text over the next few days.

[]:             Quote Post by JohnyPanic » Thu Apr 11, 2013 3:16 am NuclearLunchbox wrote: Damn, boy. That's a quality paper, right there. A few mistakes, but that's some cool stuff.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Apr 11, 2013 4:50 pm JohnyPanic wrote: Indeed not only vocabulary but visual confusion, Rebuild 2.0 messed me up.

[]:             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Fri Apr 12, 2013 12:01 am Haven't read the entire paper yet, but your observations on the relationship Evangelion has to otakus, or in this case, how Anno deal with his own inner-otaku, are dead on. Might wanna change the file name though: "Evamgelion" doesn't sound right.

[]:             Quote Post by JohnyPanic » Fri Apr 12, 2013 4:07 am Mr. Tines wrote: Anno is on record as saying that for the new movies, the character settei has been altered, and seiyuu interviews confirm that they have been directed to play the characters as being different from the original -- so it is simplest to treat the two as separate continuities perhaps having some common thematic elements (though we need to wait for the concluding movie to assess the extent of that commonality, and how much the corresponding characters turn out to play corresponding roles).
